role_summary: Black Panther Party Minister of Information (1967-1971); author of Soul on Ice (1968, McGraw-Hill); 1968 Peace and Freedom Party candidate for U.S. President; husband of Kathleen Cleaver (catalog entry 73) from 1967 until their separation in the late 1970s.

photo.alt: Eldridge Cleaver, from the Black Panther newspaper, October 5, 1968
photo.license: Public domain (U.S., published 1968 without copyright notice)

ai_reading: Eldridge Cleaver is named twice in the catalog, both within his then-wife Kathleen Cleaver's (entry 73) bio: as the spouse who shared the Algiers exile years after the April 6, 1968 Oakland shootout (the same shootout that killed Bobby Hutton, catalog entry of its own) and as the central figure of the 1971 BPP split from Huey P. Newton's wing. He is not himself a Civil Rights History Project interviewee. His standalone catalog page is added so the Kathleen Cleaver bio's narrative arc has both halves of its central marriage findable, and so the broader BPP-leadership-trio (Newton + Seale + Cleaver) has its third member as a discrete page parallel to the Newton and Seale catalog entries already in place. The novel observation his page surfaces is the post-BPP-political-trajectory inversion: Cleaver's 1968 Soul on Ice was one of the canonical Black Power texts, and by 1986 he was a Republican delegate to the California Republican Party state convention and an evangelical Christian; the catalog's BPP-cohort transcripts (Aaron Dixon, Elmer Dixon, Howard, Cleaver herself, Mtume, Hopkins) speak about him as the central political-arc puzzle of the party.
biographical_paragraph: Cleaver was born August 31, 1935 in Wabbaseka, Arkansas to sharecropping parents who moved the family to Los Angeles when he was twelve. He served prison time for marijuana possession (1953-1957) and assault with intent to kill (1957-1966), reading and writing the essays at Folsom and San Quentin that became Soul on Ice (McGraw-Hill, 1968), the autobiographical-and-polemical book that brought him to national attention. He met Bobby Seale and Huey P. Newton in 1967 and became the Black Panther Party's Minister of Information; he married Kathleen Neal Cleaver in San Francisco that December. The April 6, 1968 Oakland shootout in which seventeen-year-old Bobby Hutton was killed left Cleaver wounded; he fled to Cuba in late 1968 and then to Algeria, where he and Kathleen Cleaver lived in exile across the next several years, running the BPP's International Section from Algiers. His 1971 split from Newton over party direction broke the central committee. He returned to the United States in 1975 under negotiated terms, served eight months for the April 1968 shootout, and across the next two decades drifted toward Mormonism, then evangelical Christianity, then Republican politics. He died May 1, 1998 in Pomona, California at sixty-two.
